item.title: My Fire TV Stick is infinitely better ever since I enabled this hidden setting
item.itunes:title: My Fire TV Stick is infinitely better ever since I enabled this hidden setting
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
By default, Amazon locks your device down to its curated ecosystem. This means you miss out on powerful media players, custom launchers, and niche streaming tools.

However, buried deep within the settings menu lies a single, hidden toggle that strips away these restrictions and unlocks the hardware’s true potential.

My Fire TV Stick stopped feeling sluggish after I changed this setting

It's something you should do periodically for the best performance

By  Kazim Alvi

The default experience is insufficient for power users

The walled garden

Out of the box, you are completely restricted to the official Amazon Appstore. If an app isn’t hosted there, as far as your Fire Stick is concerned, it doesn’t exist. This ‘walled garden’ approach means Amazon gets to act as the ultimate gatekeeper of your entertainment. If an indie developer builds a game-changing media player, an advanced file manager, or a lightweight, ad-free video client, you can’t have it.

If an app competes too closely with Amazon’s own paid services, or if the developer simply didn’t want to jump through Amazon’s corporate hoops, it gets barred from the store. For me, the tipping point was the default home screen. It’s cluttered, aggressive, and constantly pushing sponsored content and recommendations I have zero interest in.

I wanted to install a clean, minimalist custom launcher. But Amazon obviously doesn’t host apps that let you hide their ads. It frustrates me to no end because, underneath all that heavy Amazon skin, the Fire TV Stick is just running a modified version of Android.

The solution is buried in settings

Toggle a setting in the Amazon Fire TV Stick

To break out of that frustrating sandbox, you don’t need to hack your device or do anything illegal. You just need to flip the switch that unlocks it. Because Fire OS is built on top of Android, it uses standard Android application files (called .apk files). Sideloading is just the act of manually downloading one of these .apk files via a web browser or file manager and installing it directly.

To Amazon, an ‘unknown source’ isn’t necessarily a dangerous one; it’s simply any source that isn’t the official Amazon Appstore. Go through these steps.

  1. Head to Fire TV Stick Settings and scroll down to My Fire TV.
  2. Click About.
  3. Click the Fire TV menu seven times.
  4. The system displays a message No need, you are already a developer.
  5. Move back to the My Fire TV
  6. You should see a new option called Developer Options. Open it.
  7. Select Apps from Unknown Sources and confirm your decision from the following menu.

That’s it — you're all set to sideload apps from other sources. The trick above is compatible with most Fire TV Stick devices. However, if you have a Fire TV Select running Vega OS, you won’t find the developer options menu.

The benefits and a word of caution

Bypassing limitations

Now that we have unlocked the system gates, it’s time to talk about how we actually get those apps onto the device. Depending on how you like to manage your files, there are several methods to get .apk files onto your TV. The easiest method is via the Downloader app. You can search for it on the Appstore and install it in no time. Once you open it, you get a simple web browser with an address bar. You just type in the direct URL of the .apk file you want to download (or use a shortened numeric code if the developer provides one). The app downloads the file and immediately prompts you to install it.

If you already have a library of installation files saved on your computer or cloud storage, you can use a third-party file manager on Fire TV Stick, connect it to the cloud storage, navigate to the saved .apk file, and let it install. When you download apps from the official Amazon Appstore, you are paying for peace of mind. Amazon constantly scans those apps for malware, data-logging scripts, and stability issues.

Because these apps don’t go through Amazon’s strict vetting process, downloading a corrupted or malicious .apk file can put your data and home network at risk. I would advise downloading apps from trusted, verified open-source repositories or official developer websites only.

Subscribe for Fire TV Stick hacks, tips, and guides

Get deeper, vetted guidance on sideloading, custom launchers, and safer Fire TV tweaks—subscribe to the newsletter for hands-on tips, trusted sources to avoid risky .apk files, and curated customization ideas that expand your streaming options.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

I have seen way too many people get excited about ‘free streaming’ apps, click on a sketchy link from a random forum or shady YouTube video description, and accidentally wreck their setup. It only takes one compromised .apk file to turn your Fire TV Stick into a sluggish, overheating nightmare. Also, be careful when streaming unlicensed content, as it may land you in legal trouble in the future.

4 often overlooked Fire TV Stick features that are super useful

Some of these features are more obvious than others, but they’re all handy.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

Unlock the full potential of Fire TV Stick

Flipping this single switch completely changes the game, transforming your Fire TV Stick from a restricted streaming device into a powerhouse. Suddenly, you have the freedom to curate your own entertainment setup exactly the way you want it. That said, you need to be careful here. While sideloading opens up endless possibilities, stepping outside Amazon’s walled garden means you are now your own digital security guard.

If you are new to a Fire TV Stick, check out these essential features for smarter streaming.

item.title: I canceled ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot subscriptions just to go back to this powerful AI tool
item.itunes:title: I canceled ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot subscriptions just to go back to this powerful AI tool
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
It’s easy to get caught up in the LLM race. For a while, my monthly credit card statement read like a lineup of premium AI subscriptions: ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, and Microsoft 365 Premium with Copilot.

But a few weeks ago, I hit cancel on all of them. The reason wasn’t subscription fatigue — it was the realization that I was paying for isolated chatbots when I could have an entire ecosystem.

While the tech world obsesses over benchmarks, Google quietly changed the game with Google AI Pro. Instead of just another chatbot, it gives you a complete workspace in one place and easily justifies its $20 price tag.

Breaking the chatbot mess

The friction factor

For months, my daily workflow felt like a chaotic game of AI hopscotch. I was trapped in a cycle of constant tab-switching. I would open ChatGPT when I needed quick, logical code snippets or technical troubleshooting.

Then, I would go over to Claude the second I needed to brainstorm and complete my vibe coding projects. If I needed to pull numbers from a spreadsheet or design a presentation from scratch, I would toggle over to Copilot.

It was exhausting. Beyond the obvious subscription fatigue of watching $20 charges rack up on my credit card statement, the real killer was context tax. Every single time I hopped between these chatbots, I had to copy, paste, and re-explain my background, project goals, and writing style to a blank text box.

Frustrated by the fragmentation, I finally decided to clear the board and give Gemini a serious shot.

The first thing that caught me was the refinement of the UI. Unlike the utilitarian text boxes of its competitors, Gemini felt like a modern workspace.

Gemini works with my favorite productivity apps

More than just Gemini 3.5 pro

The real magic started when I stopped looking at Gemini as just a text box and started treating it like the control center for my entire digital day.

It works with all the productivity, communication, and entertainment apps that I already use daily on my MacBook, Windows station, and Pixel phone — Gmail, Google Drive, Calendar, Docs, Keep, Tasks, YouTube Music, Google Photos, and even WhatsApp.

For instance, I can ask Gemini to pull up a specific pizza recipe from the web and create a note directly in Google Keep. I can even create an itinerary plan in Gemini and create a shopping list right into Google Tasks.

Gemini is also smart enough to pull up specific information right from a Google Doc document or a month-old email from Gmail.

But where it gets wild is how deep this rabbit hole actually goes once you move past basic office utilities. Before a long four-hour drive, I can just fire up Gemini on my phone or the web and ask it to create a specific playlist based on my favorite singers and save it to my library with a single click.

I also use Gemini with Google Photos. Instead of scrolling through thousands of photos, I can just ask Gemini to find me the top three photos of my family from the Ba Na Hills in Vietnam and generate a caption for my Instagram post.

Another neat Gemini trick is with WhatsApp. I can fire up Gemini on my Android phone or Android Auto and ask it to send a message with a single prompt.

It works so natively with my favorite tools that it feels less like a standalone utility and more like an intelligent layer built right into my devices.

The only things left on my ultimate wishlist are deep, native integrations with Canva and Adobe. Once those happen, the ecosystem will be completely unstoppable.

The ecosystem bundle is hard to resist

The value play

When you look at what you are actually getting for your money, the value of the Google AI Pro subscription becomes impossible to ignore.

Deals

Unlock AI & Subscription Deals to Save on Monthly Costs

Explore AI and subscription deals to cut monthly costs—discover discounts on cloud storage, premium streaming, productivity suites, and AI workspaces. Compare bundle offers to maximize savings and upgrade your workflow with software and subscription discounts.
Deals Explore Software, AI & Subscriptions Deals

Every other AI company asks for twenty bucks a month and hands you a blank chat window in return. That’s it. If you hit your message limit on Claude or ChatGPT, you are stuck waiting for a timer to reset.

Google looked at the $20 price point and decided to build an ecosystem bundle around it. The company offers 5TB Google Drive storage, which solves my cloud storage needs across all my devices.

On top of that, it bundles in Google Health Premium for deep wellness insights, and YouTube Premium Lite, which finally cuts out the annoying ad clutter while I’m watching tech breakdowns.

The subscription also unlocks massively upgraded limits in Google Antigravity. When you add it all up, none of the other tools even come close.

Ditched the big three

For me, walking away from ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot wasn’t a compromise on quality — it was an upgrade in workflow. The search giant has smartly made Google AI Pro more lucrative by bundling YouTube Premium Lite, Google Health Premium, Antigravity, and Google Drive storage.

Besides, while standalone chatbots require you to constantly copy, paste, and explain your world over and over, Gemini already lives where your work happens. Gemini in Android Auto is a beast, too.

Google Gemini

Gemini is an AI assistant that rivals ChatGPT and Claude.

See at Google Gemini

item.title: Windows won the desktop by being compatible with everything, but that's starting to look like a drawback
item.itunes:title: Windows won the desktop by being compatible with everything, but that's starting to look like a drawback
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When comparing Windows, macOS, and Linux, Microsoft's offering has one gigantic advantage: 99.9% of PC apps work on it. While Linux has made a ton of headway with Wine, and macOS has its own exclusive apps, stuff just runs on Windows the vast majority of the time. If you want to run something like Claude Code, a new video game, or any powerful app, there's an extremely good chance it has a native Windows app, and a very low chance that it has any others.

However, Windows' desire to be compatible with absolutely everything has also meant that it has to be compatible with absolutely everything. And this, unfortunately, does cause problems.

Windows will likely never become an atomic or an immutable operating system

There's just too much stuff relying on its current architecture

If you haven't dipped a toe into the world of Linux operating systems, some distros are atomic and/or immutable, usually both at the same time. Atomic means the operating system performs an A/B update process where it downloads the new system files to a new branch. When you restart, the operating system 'swaps tracks' from the old system files to the new ones, which is a lot more reliable than updating system files directly. Immutable means that nothing, not even software, can edit the system files, thus keeping the operating system clean from tampering.

These would both be amazing additions to Windows, but there's a problem. In order to bring in an atomic and/or immutable system, Microsoft would need to break decades of compatibility. For instance, it would need to scrap the Registry, which a ton of apps have relied on for decades now. And all kinds of legacy apps depend on installing or tweaking system files, which cannot be fixed by the developers simply releasing a patch for them. The dev teams may not even exist anymore.

Linux distros don't have this problem; they can add an atomic and immutable system to their software whenever they please. But Windows would be a lot harder, if not impossible, to do the same with.

Windows can never be an immutable OS, and that might be a problem

Ever wondered why it's common to reinstall Windows?

22
By  Adam Conway

Microsoft's plan for a 'jack of all trades' OS leaves it as a master of none

Other operating systems are beginning to out-Windows Windows

One of the biggest challenges Microsoft has when balancing Windows is that pretty much anyone, from any walk of life, can use the operating system for whatever they want. One person will want Windows to be the perfect professional OS, while another will want to kit it out with the latest and greatest games, and Windows needs to somehow cater to both without alienating either.

In fact, Microsoft seemingly wants Windows 11 to be the 'jack of all trades' OS. The idea is that Microsoft wouldn't need to splinter its team to make a different OS or system for different applications; instead, it can simply say "Windows 11 can handle that" and throw its flagship OS at whatever problem it's trying to solve.

The biggest example of this is with Microsoft's upcoming Xbox console. The company won't be programming a whole new operating system to run on it; instead, it'll simply add Windows 11 on it, much like it already does with its Xbox-branded handhelds. However, this does mean that the console will be running all of Windows 11 on it, including all the bits that a console really doesn't need to run.

Microsoft's solution to this is a Steam Big Picture mode-esque feature called "Xbox Fullscreen mode," where the OS rediverts all its power to rendering games and cuts through the bloat. However, we're already seeing the competition outdo Windows in gaming, a crown that Microsoft's operating system has worn for decades now. Take Bazzite, for instance: it's designed to be a lean Linux OS with no bloat, so games run smoothly without additional effort. And some games run better on Bazzite than on Windows because Bazzite's OS isn't mired in tons of systems designed to ensure it's as compatible as possible with everything.

The next Xbox console could be a Windows 11 PC in disguise, insiders claim

The Xbox isn't gone; it's just having a makeover.

10
By  Simon Batt

Windows has a huge driver-related problem that Microsoft is trying to fix

There are a lot of bad drivers out there

When Microsoft opened the doors for anything to run on Windows, it meant that anyone could get something on Windows. If a company wanted to get their hardware on the OS, they would code up a driver so that Windows knew what it was looking at. The problem is, not all drivers are created equally, and without Microsoft vetting the process or having any control over it, Windows users have ended up installing some bad or outdated drivers over the history of the OS. These cause video, audio, and even stability issues as they mess with Windows' operations.

It's a problem that has been around for decades, and something that Microsoft is only getting around to fixing. The company announced its Driver Quality Initiative to crack down on these bad binaries, and will also introduce an allow-list that may break legacy hardware. It's a problem that only arose because Windows was meant to be the OS that everything could run on, including support for old drivers.

Microsoft is finally tackling Windows 11’s driver problem

The tech giant has announced a comprehensive driver quality initiative.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

In a bid to appease everyone, Windows runs the risk of annoying everyone

For years, Microsoft has sat easy knowing that Windows was the operating system people went to for driver support, gaming performance, and app compatibility. However, as its competitors improve, Windows itself is grinding to a halt, and Microsoft needs to make some tough calls if it doesn't want to be swamped by the past.

item.title: Your old gaming PC is overkill for a home server, and that's exactly why it's perfect
item.itunes:title: Your old gaming PC is overkill for a home server, and that's exactly why it's perfect
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you’ve been a part of the PC master race for as long as I have, you’ve probably upgraded your gaming machine enough times to have some spare hardware lying around. Or maybe you skipped the Ship of Theseus situation altogether by replacing your entire system with a shiny new PC. Either way, if you’re not actively using your old gaming companion, there are a bunch of ways to breathe some new life into it – like turning it into a home server.

Interestingly enough, if you check out the minimum hardware requirements on Proxmox, TrueNAS, and other server platforms, you’d realize they can work on any ol’ PC, including dinosaur systems. But let me tell you this: the fact that your PC is overkill for server tasks is actually a good thing, as it can let you work on projects that a budget system or cheap mini-PC would struggle to handle.

I used an old gaming laptop as a home server, and it beats most entry-level mini-PCs

My outdated behemoth of a laptop doubles as a powerful server node

4
By  Ayush Pande

Gaming machines have enough system resources to power dozens of virtual guests

They may be outdated for modern titles, but LXCs and VMs are fair game

Unlike triple-A titles and typical e-sports titles, where you’ll need faster clock speeds to drive up the FPS and stay ahead of the curve, your CPU’s performance matters little in home labs. In fact, the number of cores and threads matter a lot more than boost clocks, but as long as you have a 6-core (or heck, even a 4-core) processor, your machine can easily handle several containers and a few VMs (especially if you go the CLI route). Source? Yours truly, who built a PVE server with a Ryzen 5 1600 years ago that’s somehow still good enough to run over a dozen LXCs, a GUI-laden Debian VM, and an Arch virtual machine.

If anything, you’ll need a decent amount of RAM for demanding home server tasks. But considering that we’re talking about a gaming machine, your old companion probably has at least 8GB of memory – and that’s plenty for the average tasks. For reference, I’d been using my first-gen Ryzen system with 16GB of RAM for the first couple of years, and although its 6-core, 12-thread processor sounds like a bottleneck, I’ve had plenty of situations where the memory utilization metrics would hit the red zone, while the CPU would chug along nicely at 50% usage. In fact, I recently had to toss another 16GB DDR4 stick into the machine just to get a 26B model running with MoE offloading on this nearly 10-year-old machine. While we’re on this subject.

Even your outdated GPU will come in handy for certain server projects

It can serve as a terrific LLM-hosting workstation

Besides the superior CPU and memory provisions of your gaming PC, you’ve probably got a GPU stuffed in your outdated rig, and it’s a surprisingly great addition to your makeshift workstation. On the self-hosting front, certain apps can make good use of your GPU’s processing capabilities. For example, if you’re planning to run a Frigate-powered Network Video Recorder on your server, you can arm it with your graphics card for AI-powered object detection, and it’s significantly more powerful than a CPU-only setup when you’ve got a bunch of security cameras in your home. Likewise, even a dinosaur GPU can run hardware-aided transcoding tasks in Jellyfin (or Plex, if you’re willing to toss extra money for its premium license). Even Immich can harness your GPU for machine learning-aided facial recognition, object detection, and smart search tools.

But leaving these standalone tools aside, your gaming GPU’s biggest utility lies in its ability to run LLMs. No, I’m not talking about tiny 4B, 7B, or 9B models, either. Ever since Mixture of Experts models have started making the rounds, even an old GPU can host massive LLMs, provided your gaming machine has enough RAM. Heck, I’ve deployed a Gemma-4-26B-A4B on a GTX 1080 – a decade-old GPU that’s hooked up to my ancient first-gen Ryzen rig. Considering my token-generation rates are almost always above 15t/s, you’re bound to get better performance if your graphics card is even remotely better than mine (which isn’t really that high of a bar when you’re pitting a modern mid-range card against something released ten years ago).

And that’s just LXCs and containers. On the virtual machine front, you can deploy a remote gaming VM that pairs with your GPU to run older titles, though the virtualized nature of this setup will result in slightly lower performance than a bare-metal configuration. I tend to avoid VM passthrough these days, as I’ve got multiple LXCs hooked up to my GPU, and attempting to access the same graphics card from different virtual machines is impossible on an SR-IOV-less setup such as mine.

I used an Intel N100 mini-PC as a standalone Proxmox node – it went really well

Besides being able to self-host multiple containers, it can even run a few VMs

2
By  Ayush Pande

Gaming-centric mobos tend to have plenty of expansion headers

You can arm them with several PCIe cards

Unless your outdated gaming PC has a Mini-ITX motherboard, you’re bound to have a bunch of spare PCIe ports on your system, even if your massive GPU covers more than one. While these empty sockets are fairly useful for ordinary systems, their real utility comes into light once you start building your home lab. Let’s say your aged gaming machine has 1G Ethernet capabilities. Arming it with a 2.5GbE network card can enhance its file transfer speeds, provided you have a switch that supports this standard. Having an extra Ethernet at your disposal also lets you set up virtualized firewall instances without configuring VLAN-based workarounds.

On the storage front, you can add extra SATA ports to your machine, toss in a couple of HDDs, and use it as a backup rig. Alternatively, you can use NVMe-to-PCIe adapters to use these blazing-fast drives as virtual guest storage pools instead of relying on slow hard drives.

With the right tweaks, your system won’t siphon too much energy, either

The biggest complaint about reusing old systems as servers isn’t their utility – it’s their tendency to hog extra power compared to mini-PCs and thin clients. Truth be told, outdated PCs are definitely less energy efficient than their modern counterparts. However, there are a bunch of ways you can reduce your aged gaming companion’s electricity-guzzling tendencies.

If you’re a pixel-pushing overclocker like me, you might want to turn those boost clock speeds all the way down before arming your old rig with a home server platform. Likewise, enabling Eco-Mode, ASPM, C-states, and other power-saving options may seem preposterous to FPS-chasers, but these settings can go a long way in reducing your PC’s power consumption. Finally, if you’re on Proxmox, I’d recommend setting the CPU scaling governor to powersave, as it can massively cut the idle wattage of your makeshift home server.

Proxmox
See at Proxmox

Besides the superior CPU and memory provisions of your gaming PC, you’ve probably got a GPU stuffed in your outdated rig, and it’s a surprisingly great addition to your makeshift workstation. On the self-hosting front, certain apps can make good use of your GPU’s processing capabilities. For example, if you’re planning to run a Frigate-powered Network Video Recorder on your server, you can arm it with your graphics card for AI-powered object detection, and it’s significantly more powerful than a CPU-only setup when you’ve got a bunch of security cameras in your home. Likewise, even a dinosaur GPU can run hardware-aided transcoding tasks in Jellyfin (or Plex, if you’re willing to toss extra money for its premium license). Even Immich can harness your GPU for machine learning-aided facial recognition, object detection, and smart search tools.

But leaving these standalone tools aside, your gaming GPU’s biggest utility lies in its ability to run LLMs. No, I’m not talking about tiny 4B, 7B, or 9B models, either. Ever since Mixture of Experts models have started making the rounds, even an old GPU can host massive LLMs, provided your gaming machine has enough RAM. Heck, I’ve deployed a Gemma-4-26B-A4B on a GTX 1080 – a decade-old GPU that’s hooked up to my ancient first-gen Ryzen rig. Considering my token-generation rates are almost always above 15t/s, you’re bound to get better performance if your graphics card is even remotely better than mine (which isn’t really that high of a bar when you’re pitting a modern mid-range card against something released ten years ago).

And that’s just LXCs and containers. On the virtual machine front, you can deploy a remote gaming VM that pairs with your GPU to run older titles, though the virtualized nature of this setup will result in slightly lower performance than a bare-metal configuration. I tend to avoid VM passthrough these days, as I’ve got multiple LXCs hooked up to my GPU, and attempting to access the same graphics card from different virtual machines is impossible on an SR-IOV-less setup such as mine.

I used an Intel N100 mini-PC as a standalone Proxmox node – it went really well

Besides being able to self-host multiple containers, it can even run a few VMs

2
By  Ayush Pande

Gaming-centric mobos tend to have plenty of expansion headers

You can arm them with several PCIe cards

Unless your outdated gaming PC has a Mini-ITX motherboard, you’re bound to have a bunch of spare PCIe ports on your system, even if your massive GPU covers more than one. While these empty sockets are fairly useful for ordinary systems, their real utility comes into light once you start building your home lab. Let’s say your aged gaming machine has 1G Ethernet capabilities. Arming it with a 2.5GbE network card can enhance its file transfer speeds, provided you have a switch that supports this standard. Having an extra Ethernet at your disposal also lets you set up virtualized firewall instances without configuring VLAN-based workarounds.

On the storage front, you can add extra SATA ports to your machine, toss in a couple of HDDs, and use it as a backup rig. Alternatively, you can use NVMe-to-PCIe adapters to use these blazing-fast drives as virtual guest storage pools instead of relying on slow hard drives.

With the right tweaks, your system won’t siphon too much energy, either

The biggest complaint about reusing old systems as servers isn’t their utility – it’s their tendency to hog extra power compared to mini-PCs and thin clients. Truth be told, outdated PCs are definitely less energy efficient than their modern counterparts. However, there are a bunch of ways you can reduce your aged gaming companion’s electricity-guzzling tendencies.

If you’re a pixel-pushing overclocker like me, you might want to turn those boost clock speeds all the way down before arming your old rig with a home server platform. Likewise, enabling Eco-Mode, ASPM, C-states, and other power-saving options may seem preposterous to FPS-chasers, but these settings can go a long way in reducing your PC’s power consumption. Finally, if you’re on Proxmox, I’d recommend setting the CPU scaling governor to powersave, as it can massively cut the idle wattage of your makeshift home server.

Proxmox
See at Proxmox

item.title: I made Claude Code worse by giving it too much freedom, and here's how to keep it laser focused
item.itunes:title: I made Claude Code worse by giving it too much freedom, and here's how to keep it laser focused
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I don't really need to convince you that AI tools go haywire pretty easily. You ask them a simple question, they give you an answer that answers nothing you actually asked. You ask them to tidy up your desktop, they wipe the whole thing. At a company called PocketOS, a Claude-powered coding agent casually deleted the entire production database in nine seconds when doing a routine check. When the founder asked what had happened, the agent confessed that they had guessed instead of verifying it. All that to say, an AI agent going haywire and making bad decisions is a very real concern right now.

With how great these AI coding tools have become, it's tempting to keep handing them more responsibility. Giving them bigger tasks, looser briefs with vaguer instructions, and just letting them run. That's the move most of us have been making, and it feels like the right one. Unfortunately, it's also the move that quietly breaks them. When it comes to Claude Code specifically, here are a couple of ways I've been keeping it laser-focused.

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

It begins with understanding that the problem isn't Claude Code

No setting fixes a vague brief

Before I talk about the Claude Code settings specifically I tweak, there's something more fundamental worth saying: most of the time, when Claude Code goes off the rails, the problem is often how you're using it. With AI tools, the model will do roughly what you let it do. If you hand it a vague task and full autonomy, it will improvise. Sometimes it'll improvise brilliantly, sometimes catastrophically, and you don't really get to pick which. Sure, there are settings that limit what an AI agent will go ahead and do, but the onus is ultimately on you.

So, it's extremely important that you get the absolute basics right. Be extremely specific with what you want, and equally specific about what you don't want. If you can't describe your requirements properly and don't really know what you want, Claude Code (as will any other tool) will fill in the gaps itself, and you've already lost control of the output before the first token is generated. With the mindset out of the way, here's what I actually tweak.

Plan mode does most of the work

Not just at the start

The easiest way to keep Claude Code in control is using Plan mode. If you aren't familiar with it, Plan mode puts Claude into a read-only state where it can look at your code, ask questions, and propose a step-by-step plan. The twist is that it can't actually edit anything in this mode. It can't run commands or touch your files until you switch out of Plan mode. You can toggle into it by pressing Shift + Tab twice, and you exit the same way once you're happy with what it's proposing.

The reason this matters is that most of the catastrophic things Claude Code does happen in the first thirty seconds of a task, before you've had a chance to course-correct. It reads your prompt, makes some assumptions about what you want, and starts confidently executing on those assumptions. By the time you notice it's heading in the wrong direction, it's already three files deep. Plan mode forces a pause before any of that happens, and you get to see exactly what Claude thinks you're asking for, and exactly how it intends to go about it, before a single line of code changes.

An important thing I want to mention here is that I've seen a lot of people use Plan mode, but they only use it at the very beginning of their project. They'll plan out the initial scaffolding, get a clean implementation going, and then once execution starts, they never go back into Plan mode for the rest of the session. I think this is the biggest mistake you can make! Plan mode isn't strictly a setup step — it's the tool you should be reaching for any time the task in front of you stops being trivial. Especially when you're debugging something!

Claude Code works best when you stop asking it to code

Claude Code became far more useful once I stopped treating it like a code generator and started using it to understand projects and terminal chaos.

11
By  Jeff Butts

CLAUDE.md matters more than you think it does

Write it like you mean it

Another non-negotiable for me is creating a CLAUDE.md file for every single project I work on within Claude Code. If you haven't come across one before, CLAUDE.md is a markdown file that sits at the root of your project, and Claude Code reads it automatically at the start of every session. It's where you put the rules, conventions, and do's/dont's you want Claude to carry into every task. This includes files you never want it to touch, commit message styles, tech stacks you want it to use, naming conventions for variables and functions, and basically any decisions you've made that you don't want to repeat again and again.

Without a CLAUDE.md, you're relying entirely on the prompt you wrote seconds ago to carry all of that context. Even if you remember to include the important constraints every single time (which you won't), you're burning tokens and attention on stuff that should be ambient. So, before I begin any project, I run the /init command to auto-generate a CLAUDE.md file. I then add on to this file as I go. Every time Claude does something I don't want it to do, it goes into the file. It edited something I told it to leave alone? Into the file. It rewrote a clean function in a style I don't use? Into the file.

Over a few weeks of working like this, the CLAUDE.md naturally grows into something that reflects how the project actually works, not just how it looks on the surface. That said, it's important you make sure the CLAUDE.md file doesn't get too long and stays confined to the rules that matter most. A bloated CLAUDE.md is almost as bad as not having one!

I use the /clear command fairly often

A clean slate is cheaper than a wasted hour

Given that a huge topic of conversation has been rate limits on AI tools lately, you've likely heard people advising you to use features like Projects and essentially switch to new conversations as often as you can. The logic here is typically that shorter sessions burn fewer tokens per turn, the model performs better on focused context, and the LLM at hand no longer needs to read through the entire previous conversation to answer your next question. That advice holds up in regular Claude conversations, but it's even more important in Claude Code, because the consequences of a bloated session aren't just slower responses or worse answers. They're now also Claude editing the wrong file because it's still half-remembering a constraint from a task you finished an hour ago.

The /clear slash command is how you act on this inside Claude Code. It wipes your current session entirely. Now, a lot of people either don't use this command or use it only when things have gone sideways. The thing is, by the time a session is bad enough that you need to clear, you've already burned thirty minutes on output that was quietly degrading for the last twenty. I treat /clear as the move I make between tasks, not the move I make after one has gone sideways. Finished a feature? /clear. About to start something that has nothing to do with the last thing? /clear, every time. This way, you'll be able to keep your limits under control and also keep Claude sharper for the task actually in front of you!

Configure permissions before you need them

Don't trade safety for fewer clicks

Claude Code asks you for permission before it does anything potentially destructive, like running a shell command, editing a file, or hitting the network. By default, that means a lot of "approve" clicks throughout a session, and it's the friction that pushes most people toward the --dangerously-skip-permissions flag. Now, you shouldn't really do that. There's a reason why Anthropic literally called that mode dangerous.

Subscribe: AI Insider Newsletter for practical Claude Code tips

Get practical AI tool coverage—subscribe to the XDA AI Insider newsletter for hands-on deep dives, tool recommendations, and concrete guidance on safety and best practices for Claude Code and other coding agents.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The whole point of permissions is that they're the last thing standing between Claude and a destroyed file, a force-pushed branch, or a wiped database (which is exactly the PocketOS scenario I described at the beginning). What you actually want is a middle ground. You can open up .claude/settings.json and whitelist the commands you genuinely don't need to be asked about. This way, you stop clicking "allow" fifty times a session for harmless commands, and the real safety net is still there for the commands that matter.

It still comes down to you

Again, at the end of the day, it is ultimately on you. Claude Code can only be as focused as the constraints you put around it!

item.title: Forget hunting for working CRTs, this is how I nailed the retro look for my emulation catalog
item.itunes:title: Forget hunting for working CRTs, this is how I nailed the retro look for my emulation catalog
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • RetroArch CRT shaders add scanlines, glow and blur to make retro games feel like they did on CRTs.
  • They're built-in: F1 > Quick Menu > Shaders > Load Presets. Many CRT, handheld, and 'Lite/Fast' options exist.
  • CRT shaders restore blur, color bleed, and soft edges, making old RPGs and sprites feel cozy and playable longer.

It's always exciting to wake up one day and remember a game you used to play. You download it immediately, but the joy lasts only 10 minutes until the first Game Over screen, after which you promptly forget about it. Part of that comes down to how these old games were actually meant to look. There were a lot of rough edges in older games that motion blur, pixel art, and the softness of CRT displays helped to mask. Playing them on a razor-sharp modern monitor can sometimes make them feel... sterile.

I was absolutely certain that buying a used CRT monitor might be the key to fixing that and to keeping me engaged with these classics a lot longer. Sadly, the legwork involved got the better of me, both in bargaining and in setting things up. As such, I figured I could get the CRT feel with RetroArch itself, and what do you know, it absolutely worked.

I didn’t expect this GPU-powered SNES emulator to make 16-bit games look this good

Super ZSNES gives classic Super Nintendo games a more modern look.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

RetroArch's shader presets take you right back to the CRT era

Retro games were never meant to look this sharp

To get the old-timey feel while playing old classics from the SNES, PS1, and PS2 eras, it's important to understand that simply playing them through an emulator frontend like RetroArch isn't enough. For the most part, RetroArch does get the job done — it gets all your games in one place, lets you emulate multiple consoles from over the years, and even manages different control schemes and save states for each game. And yet, the problem of classic games looking over-sharpened or sterile remains. This would be a problem even if you used individual emulators like SNES9x or DuckStation, since your display would remain the same.

Emulators come with different graphics settings, but there's a special feature in RetroArch that truly makes things easier on the eyes, and then some. Enter shader presets. In RetroArch, you can emulate your games, sure, but you can even simulate displays as well, and that's through Shader Presets. There's a massive collection of shader presets in RetroArch that can mimic everything from curved CRT TVs and fuzzy scanlines to handheld LCD ghosting, phosphor glow, and color bleed. It can even emulate old-school dithering tricks, making retro games look far closer to how they actually felt back in the day.

One of the most popular and proven shader presets is CRT_Royale. It goes absurdly deep in recreating the quirks of old CRT televisions. It somehow makes ancient pixel art feel less like an emulator running on a PC and more like a memory coming back to life. Of course, since these are GPU-powered presets, they do come with a slight performance cost. This resource tax doesn't matter all that much if you're on already-powerful hardware. However, for those with modest GPUs and CPUs, most CRT shader presets also have "Lite" or "Fast" versions that achieve the same effect without slowing your games down to a halt.

Setting up RetroArch on my iPhone turned it into the perfect gaming handheld

A few generations' worth of backlog, one cab ride at a time

By  Samarveer Singh

CRT presets in RetroArch are easy to install

Turn your high-end OLED monitor into a CRT display

You don't have to install CRT shaders in RetroArch. They come packaged with the app, so all you really have to do is toggle them on. Once you have your games and emulators set up in RetroArch, simply run any game you like. Pause it once, and bring up the Quick Menu by pressing F1. From there, head to Shaders, and toggle Video Shaders on. Now, scroll down to Load Presets, and the Shaders directory will open. This is where you make your selection. On most modern systems, the folder you're looking for should be named shaders_slang.

You'll notice that things get surprisingly deep here. You'll find entire folders dedicated to CRT-style presets, handheld-inspired filters, arcade display simulations, and even lighter, faster variants for people running older or lower-end GPUs. All you have to do is apply one and back out of the menu, and you'll see the difference instantly. Instead of a decades-old ROM stretched out over a 27- or 32-inch monitor, you'll see the game like your brain actually remembers it from all those years ago.

Emulation has become so easy that the setup is completely invisible now

It feels just like a console.

4
By  João Carrasqueira

CRT shaders restore the look retro games were designed around

Old pixel art was built for blur and scanlines

It shouldn't come as a surprise that seeing raw pixels on your monitor today while playing retro games isn't how the original developers intended it back then. Older games from the SNES, Genesis, PS1, and even the PS2 era were designed around CRT displays first and foremost. With that in mind, they took scanlines, analog blur, and natural color bleeding all into account to create the final image we all remember seeing on the screen. In fact, developers actively relied on that blending effect to create softer edges, fake transparency, gradients, and shadows, all before they could technically exist.

Modern LED panels, however, completely break that illusion, leading to unusually sharp edges where you can see every pixel turning into a perfectly defined square. That's why our old games can look a lot harsher than they were ever supposed to. Sprite outlines will become jagged, and the backgrounds will lose their softness, with certain visual tricks simply refusing to work. CRT shaders help address that problem by reintroducing visual elements exclusive to CRT displays, such as blur, scanline merging, subtle glow, and analog imperfections.

RetroArch

RetroArch is a cross-platform emulator frontend that supports many different emulators. It lets you play classic games on modern computers with a single interface.

See at RetroArch See at Steam
This free tool on Steam just gave me a reason to reinstall every single game I've ever played

ShaderGlass is a free tool you can download on Steam that will rewire the way you look at your entire library game.

12
By  Samarveer Singh

Making retro games look "worse" is what makes them look right

Truth be told, the CRT shaders in RetroArch don't exactly replicate the old CRT displays with high technical accuracy. This method won't turn your monitor into a Sony Trinitron, but it will make old games feel the way they did when we first played them. Our brains remember these games as warm, slightly blurry, and softer around the edges. RetroArch's CRT shaders make them feel just that — familiar.

Deals

Save on gaming gear to boost retro playstyle

Explore gaming gear deals and discounts to recreate authentic retro visuals and comfort — find savings on low-latency displays, retro-style controllers, capture devices, HDMI scalers, and accessories to upgrade your emulation setup affordably.
Deals Explore Gaming Gear Deals

Plus, this beats lugging around a 30-pound CRT monitor in and out of my room. Now, this could still be subjective, but using CRT shaders on my retro games makes old RPG towns feel cozy again, and that's what keeps me playing them longer instead of simply quitting RetroArch after a handful of minutes. It's definitely a psychological and nostalgic factor, but it absolutely does help recreate chasing coins, traveling through dimensions with Link, or beating up thugs in Double Dragon, exactly the way my five-year-old self remembers it.
item.description:
Summary

  • RetroArch CRT shaders add scanlines, glow and blur to make retro games feel like they did on CRTs.
  • They're built-in: F1 > Quick Menu > Shaders > Load Presets. Many CRT, handheld, and 'Lite/Fast' options exist.
  • CRT shaders restore blur, color bleed, and soft edges, making old RPGs and sprites feel cozy and playable longer.

It's always exciting to wake up one day and remember a game you used to play. You download it immediately, but the joy lasts only 10 minutes until the first Game Over screen, after which you promptly forget about it. Part of that comes down to how these old games were actually meant to look. There were a lot of rough edges in older games that motion blur, pixel art, and the softness of CRT displays helped to mask. Playing them on a razor-sharp modern monitor can sometimes make them feel... sterile.

I was absolutely certain that buying a used CRT monitor might be the key to fixing that and to keeping me engaged with these classics a lot longer. Sadly, the legwork involved got the better of me, both in bargaining and in setting things up. As such, I figured I could get the CRT feel with RetroArch itself, and what do you know, it absolutely worked.

I didn’t expect this GPU-powered SNES emulator to make 16-bit games look this good

Super ZSNES gives classic Super Nintendo games a more modern look.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

RetroArch's shader presets take you right back to the CRT era

Retro games were never meant to look this sharp

To get the old-timey feel while playing old classics from the SNES, PS1, and PS2 eras, it's important to understand that simply playing them through an emulator frontend like RetroArch isn't enough. And yet, the problem of classic games looking over-sharpened or sterile remains. This would be a problem even if you used individual emulators like SNES9x or DuckStation, since your display would remain the same.

Emulators come with different graphics settings, but there's a special feature in RetroArch that truly makes things easier on the eyes, and then some. Enter shader presets. In RetroArch, you can emulate your games, sure, but you can even simulate displays as well, and that's through Shader Presets. It can even emulate old-school dithering tricks, making retro games look far closer to how they actually felt back in the day.

One of the most popular and proven shader presets is CRT_Royale. It goes absurdly deep in recreating the quirks of old CRT televisions. It somehow makes ancient pixel art feel less like an emulator running on a PC and more like a memory coming back to life. However, for those with modest GPUs and CPUs, most CRT shader presets also have "Lite" or "Fast" versions that achieve the same effect without slowing your games down to a halt.

On most modern systems, the folder you're looking for should be named shaders_slang.

You'll notice that things get surprisingly deep here. It feels just like a console.

That's why our old games can look a lot harsher than they were ever supposed to. See at RetroArch See at Steam

item.title: I built a local voice assistant that doesn't need the internet, and it actually understands me
item.itunes:title: I built a local voice assistant that doesn't need the internet, and it actually understands me
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Any smart home user knows the familiar irritation of asking a cloud-based smart speaker to turn off a desk lamp. You will find that the audio is recorded, shipped over the internet to a corporate data center, processed, and then sent back. When it fails, you get the spinning red or orange ring of doom and a robotic voice complaining about connection issues. If it succeeds, you will find that it took so much longer to turn off your desk lamp than if you had just gotten up and done it yourself.

Contrast this with a local setup where you give a command to a tiny, non-monetized microphone on your desk. The local lights blink instantly, and a natural-sounding voice replies without a single packet ever exiting your front door.

Building a private voice assistant used to mean compromising on accuracy or typing endlessly into Linux terminal configurations. However, thanks to recent engineering leaps in Home Assistant's local voice engine, you can now build an entirely local, offline smart speaker that matches the speed and intent comprehension of big tech alternatives for zero monthly fees.

Local LLMs changed how I use Home Assistant, and now my smart devices actually listen

Local LLMs made my Home Assistant setup far more responsive than any app or integration

3
By  Ayush Pande

The software trilogy

Your new voice assistant can actually understand you

The anatomy of an offline voice stack needs to be understood before you get started. There are three open-source software pillars running on the server backend to make this possible. Pillar number one is your speech-to-text software. An example might be Whisper. OpenAI's open-source Whisper model, specifically optimized variants like Faster-Whisper, convert raw spoken audio into text data. Of course, the hardware matters here too, as a Raspberry Pi 4 takes six to eight seconds to parse a sentence, but an affordable Intel N100 mini PC completes it in under 300ms.

Pillar number two is intent recognition, but this can be done through a Home Assistant assist. Using Home Assistant Assist takes your speech from just words to a model that actually understands you. Instead of an LLM guessing wildly and hallucinating commands, ASSIST relies on a native, hard-coded, phrase-matching blueprint. It executes the automation instantly because it has direct local access to your smart home database.

And the third and final pillar to complete the Holy Trinity is your text-to-speech. This will allow your new smart home assistant to actually respond to you. Piper is a great option for this, as it's a hyper-optimized local neural text-to-speech engine. Unlike the choppy, robotic, legacy Linux voices of the past, Piper generates human-like, high-fidelity vocal responses locally, using minimal system memory.

You'll need hardware, too

And it's significantly cheaper than an Amazon Echo

Of course, alongside all of the software you have to implement, the hardware is fundamental too. Physical microphones in your home can capture voice commands within rooms without relying on expensive hardware. Devices like the Home Assistant Voice Preview Edition or even custom M5 Stack ATOM Echo boards run entirely on cheap ESP32-S3 microcontrollers via ESPHome.

By pairing an ESP32-S3 microcontroller with a dual-mic array like the XMOS one, you get your hardware satellite. Add on a physical mute switch, and you've got yourself the absolute perfect setup. While it might not look the most peachy, you could always 3D print a case to keep all of this in, so it looks a little flashier when you've got it on your desk or in your kitchen.

A physical hardware toggle switch that literally cuts the electrical power trace to the microphone can be so fundamental. It's a security barrier built on physics rather than code. The satellite has no operating system or local storage and can only stream raw audio data over your local Wi-Fi when triggered by its local wake-word engine.

How to get set up

It's an easier process than you think

So once you've got your software stack and your hardware satellite, you're ready to get started. The first thing is to deploy the voice add-ons. Navigate to your Home Assistant settings panel and install the local Whisper and Piper containers via the add-on store. Allow these containers to install and ensure that they boot correctly before proceeding to the next step.

Deals

Save on smart home deals: discounts for local voice gear

Explore discounts across smart home & gadget deals to build a local voice assistant—score savings on microphones, ESP32 mic satellites, mini PCs, mic arrays, hubs, and 3D-printing accessories to assemble a private, offline system.
Deals Explore Smart Home & Gadgets Deals

Next is Bind via the Wyoming Protocol. Once the containers are booted, Home Assistant should automatically discover them using the Wyoming integration, an ultra-lightweight communication protocol designed to pass raw audio buffers between local servers. And now you're ready to construct your voice assistant profile. Go to your Settings, select Voice Assistants, and create a new pipeline profile. Map your Speech to text field to Whisper and set your Conversation Agent to Home Assistant. Choose your preferred local voice profile in Piper.

Next, it's time to flash and adopt your microphone. Plug your ESP32-S3 voice satellite into your computer over USB. Use a browser-based ESP home tool to flash the native voice firmware over the web wire. Connect it to your local Wi-Fi subnet and adopt it into your dashboard. With that, you're ready to go. The hardware you've created can be used just like standard smart home hubs and voice assistants, such as Google Assistant and Alexa.

Stop relying on big tech's servers

Get rid of the cloud altogether

Your home shouldn't depend on an internet connection or a corporate privacy policy just to switch off a bedside lamp. Instead of letting big tech monitor your voice data and drop support for your aging smart speakers, spend an afternoon setting up a local Whisper-and-Piper pipeline. Scatter a few cheap ESP32 mic satellites around your home and claim absolute local sovereignty over your infrastructure.

Contrast this with a local setup where you give a command to a tiny, non-monetized microphone on your desk. The local lights blink instantly, and a natural-sounding voice replies without a single packet ever exiting your front door.

Building a private voice assistant used to mean compromising on accuracy or typing endlessly into Linux terminal configurations. However, thanks to recent engineering leaps in Home Assistant's local voice engine, you can now build an entirely local, offline smart speaker that matches the speed and intent comprehension of big tech alternatives for zero monthly fees.

Local LLMs changed how I use Home Assistant, and now my smart devices actually listen

Local LLMs made my Home Assistant setup far more responsive than any app or integration

3
By  Ayush Pande

The software trilogy

Your new voice assistant can actually understand you

The anatomy of an offline voice stack needs to be understood before you get started. There are three open-source software pillars running on the server backend to make this possible. Pillar number one is your speech-to-text software. An example might be Whisper. OpenAI's open-source Whisper model, specifically optimized variants like Faster-Whisper, convert raw spoken audio into text data. Of course, the hardware matters here too, as a Raspberry Pi 4 takes six to eight seconds to parse a sentence, but an affordable Intel N100 mini PC completes it in under 300ms.

Pillar number two is intent recognition, but this can be done through a Home Assistant assist. Using Home Assistant Assist takes your speech from just words to a model that actually understands you. Instead of an LLM guessing wildly and hallucinating commands, ASSIST relies on a native, hard-coded, phrase-matching blueprint. It executes the automation instantly because it has direct local access to your smart home database.

And the third and final pillar to complete the Holy Trinity is your text-to-speech. This will allow your new smart home assistant to actually respond to you. Piper is a great option for this, as it's a hyper-optimized local neural text-to-speech engine. Unlike the choppy, robotic, legacy Linux voices of the past, Piper generates human-like, high-fidelity vocal responses locally, using minimal system memory.

You'll need hardware, too

And it's significantly cheaper than an Amazon Echo

Of course, alongside all of the software you have to implement, the hardware is fundamental too. Physical microphones in your home can capture voice commands within rooms without relying on expensive hardware. Devices like the Home Assistant Voice Preview Edition or even custom M5 Stack ATOM Echo boards run entirely on cheap ESP32-S3 microcontrollers via ESPHome.

By pairing an ESP32-S3 microcontroller with a dual-mic array like the XMOS one, you get your hardware satellite. Add on a physical mute switch, and you've got yourself the absolute perfect setup. While it might not look the most peachy, you could always 3D print a case to keep all of this in, so it looks a little flashier when you've got it on your desk or in your kitchen.

A physical hardware toggle switch that literally cuts the electrical power trace to the microphone can be so fundamental. It's a security barrier built on physics rather than code. The satellite has no operating system or local storage and can only stream raw audio data over your local Wi-Fi when triggered by its local wake-word engine.

How to get set up

It's an easier process than you think

So once you've got your software stack and your hardware satellite, you're ready to get started. The first thing is to deploy the voice add-ons. Navigate to your Home Assistant settings panel and install the local Whisper and Piper containers via the add-on store. Allow these containers to install and ensure that they boot correctly before proceeding to the next step.

Deals

Save on smart home deals: discounts for local voice gear

Explore discounts across smart home & gadget deals to build a local voice assistant—score savings on microphones, ESP32 mic satellites, mini PCs, mic arrays, hubs, and 3D-printing accessories to assemble a private, offline system.
Deals Explore Smart Home & Gadgets Deals

Next is Bind via the Wyoming Protocol. Once the containers are booted, Home Assistant should automatically discover them using the Wyoming integration, an ultra-lightweight communication protocol designed to pass raw audio buffers between local servers. And now you're ready to construct your voice assistant profile. Go to your Settings, select Voice Assistants, and create a new pipeline profile. Map your Speech to text field to Whisper and set your Conversation Agent to Home Assistant. Choose your preferred local voice profile in Piper.

Next, it's time to flash and adopt your microphone. Plug your ESP32-S3 voice satellite into your computer over USB. Use a browser-based ESP home tool to flash the native voice firmware over the web wire. Connect it to your local Wi-Fi subnet and adopt it into your dashboard. With that, you're ready to go. The hardware you've created can be used just like standard smart home hubs and voice assistants, such as Google Assistant and Alexa.

Stop relying on big tech's servers

Get rid of the cloud altogether

Your home shouldn't depend on an internet connection or a corporate privacy policy just to switch off a bedside lamp. Instead of letting big tech monitor your voice data and drop support for your aging smart speakers, spend an afternoon setting up a local Whisper-and-Piper pipeline. Scatter a few cheap ESP32 mic satellites around your home and claim absolute local sovereignty over your infrastructure.

item.title: I've started sharing NotebookLM notebooks with friends instead of just my notes
item.itunes:title: I've started sharing NotebookLM notebooks with friends instead of just my notes
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
NotebookLM has long exceeded expectations in the functionality department, making it easier to use than any other publicly available LLM with user-added guardrails to perform similarly. Although this AI tool shares the Gemini LLMs with other Gemini-branded Google utilities, its standout feature remains minimal hallucinated info in results because each notebook is limited to the sources you're supplying.

At I/O in May, Google announced a switch to an AI-first Search experience, which inevitably opens up room for incorrect or unvetted data in AI summaries and AI Mode results that retain access to the wider internet. To me, this only makes NotebookLM an even more powerful thing, because I can limit it to expert sources of my choosing. However, there's one criminally underrated feature in NotebookLM that helps it take the fight to all that AI in Search, at least for your friends — sharing these notebooks.

4 reasons Open Notebook is the best self-hosted NotebookLM alternative

No need to share your research data with Google anymore

2
By  Ayush Pande

Discussions get repetitive

I'd rather have AI preach from the same sources that taught me

I grew up in the age where sharing a notebook meant passing a physically stapled stack of paper, but I've lost count of how many times I've tediously retraced my search journey to share a Reddit discussion with the tenth friend to bring up a certain hot topic in conversation. After a point, it gets tiring because it happens often in hobbyist communities where I'm active. I enjoy sharing what I know, but sometimes, I just feel lazy to repeat myself for the umpteenth time explaining how someone's NAS would benefit from an *arr stack, or how to correctly re-paste a CPU.

A bunch of these topics are better explained through video and articles like this one, so I recently thought of throwing all these frequently accessed resources into a handful of NotebookLM notebooks. The individual sources would be shelved in a searchable repository, and I can gladly point people asking repetitive questions to the Notebook's chatbot instead of a snarky Let-me-Google-that-for-you link.

They'd stand to benefit from quickly getting up to speed on a topic, and Google AI should also demonstrate infinite reserves of patience for back-and-forth questioning. Of course, I'd be just a message away to fill in the holes of an AI-generated response. As such, these notebooks become successful starter resources for hobbyist communities where new members are likely to ask the same dozen-odd questions. Pointing them to a resource beats ghosting them, and also opens up human-to-human chatrooms for more unique, insightful conversations.

Sharing isn't a new feature

It's just super underrated

All this relies on the convenience of sharing the notebooks I'm curating, and hopefully, others returning the gesture. Perhaps most importantly, sharing notebooks is not a new feature for NotebookLM, and you can just hit the Share option on your creation like any other Google Workspace tool. You also get to see analytics, like date-wise user count and their number of queries to the book's AI.

The sharing controls greatly resemble Sheets, Docs, and Slides. You can email invited collaborators, or just change access to anyone with the link. In the latter configuration, visitors can quiz the chatbot based on the sources, but adding or removing them isn't possible. All this is possible right in the free tier, and you don't need to be a Google AI Pro subscriber for sharing your creations or using notebooks others give you access to.

It's easy to see how shared AI-powered topical notebooks can be an immense resource for students, researchers, and academicians alike. A shared notebook fed lecture notes, the recommended textbooks for the course in EPUB format, and explainer videos gleaned from search, can be an invaluable asset to an entire class or batch of students prepping for finals. With the Studio tools, one could create entire study guides, flashcards for effective memorization, and unit-wise reports, in addition to quick doubt resolution using the chatbot. By default, Google AI isolates each user's chatbot usage, so others with access to the same notebook won't see your conversation history either.

I can’t believe it took NotebookLM this long to add public links — here’s how I’m using them

A minor feature, but a major win.

3
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Get started with featured notebooks

Limited selection with limitless potential

If you're still unsure of how you could use sharing in NotebookLM, the Featured Notebooks section is a great starting point, available right on the project landing page. I'll admit the selection is limited, but the tech titan has partnered with reputable industry leaders to curate notebooks of various kinds, mostly to showcase NotebookLM's capabilities.

Deals

Save on software & AI deals: subscriptions and tools

Discover deals and discounts on software, AI tools, and subscription plans to power shared notebooks, study resources, and research workflows. Find savings on productivity apps, cloud storage, collaboration suites, and learning subscriptions to streamline teamwork and study.
Deals Explore Software, AI & Subscriptions Deals

Among these, you'll find a study guide for AP World History, made by OpenStax. This is the best example of creating your own AI-powered course refresher notebooks. You could add relevant EPUB files to your document, lecture notes, resources shared among your peers, and transform the notebook into a shareable repository of authentic, peer-vetted information about the subject in question. Shared or not, with the skill to curate your own resources for everything, you can save time and effort hunting down answers curated by subject-matter experts.

NotebookLM


NotebookLM is Google’s AI-powered research assistant that turns your uploaded documents, notes, and sources into an intelligent, conversational workspace that helps you connect ideas, summarize insights, and generate new ones.

See at NotebookLM

item.title: Windows XP wasn't better, it was just simpler — and Windows 11 forgot that lesson
item.itunes:title: Windows XP wasn't better, it was just simpler — and Windows 11 forgot that lesson
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Windows XP has been out of support for over 12 years now. Despite that, people still run Windows XP, and many fondly remember it as one of the best Windows iterations to date. It wasn't the most secure or even the most powerful, if we look at it in present terms, but there was something unique about Windows XP that every discussion about Windows somehow circles back to. If you ask me, it was simplicity that made Windows XP a hit.

I agree, there were limitations, far too many to be honest, but the OS was built around the end user. It was designed to cater to us, not push services and apps Microsoft deemed appropriate. It was a much cleaner OS where everything felt practical. The operating system actually listened to you instead of constantly recommending things. I have been on Windows 11 since the early days, and I have never felt the same way about it. It's undoubtedly more modern-looking, more secure, and can do a lot more, but it's no match for Windows XP when I objectively evaluate it on simplicity.

Windows XP was easier to work with

From launching utilities to changing settings

One of the biggest drawbacks with Windows 11, and what Windows XP excelled at, is making everything easier to access. The Start Menu was way more straightforward, and everything you needed was right there. You opened the Start menu, and within a few clicks, you could access almost anything. On Windows 11, the Start menu is somewhat of a mess, though for frequently used built-in utilities, we do have the Quick Links menu, accessible via the Windows + X keys. That's a slight relief.

Moving on to configuring settings, Windows XP was again more straightforward. Every entry within the Control Panel was pretty much self-explanatory, eliminating the need for guesswork. On Windows 11, the Settings app looks more modern, but many important options require more clicks to access than they ideally should on a user-centric operating system. Let's take changing default apps as an example. On Windows XP, you open the Start menu, go to "Set Program Access and Defaults," and then select default programs for entire tasks. In Windows 11, you have to set defaults by file type, which is a major inconvenience for the average user.

As surprising as it may sound, Windows XP was even better at customizing the OS, at least the Start menu. On Windows 11, you have far fewer options. If you ever checked the "Taskbar and Start Menu Properties" on Windows XP, you could choose the size of icons, set the number of frequently accessed programs to be listed, and configure the style in which items appeared. This level of customization is not natively available in Windows 11, and you are forced to use third-party apps to customize the Start menu.

5 ways Windows has gotten worse in recent years

It's not all good news.

13
By  Simon Batt

Everything worked naturally on Windows XP

No constant distractions and less confusion

Even someone who has never used a Windows PC would have no trouble figuring out Windows XP. On Windows 11, it's a different story altogether. My biggest complaint with Windows 11 is the co-existence of the Settings app and Control Panel. I, for one, favor the Control Panel, but the messy integration is what bothers me. You click on something in the Settings, and you are automatically redirected to the Control Panel. Similarly, click an option in Control Panel, and Windows instantly opens Settings. This has reached a point where it starts to feel frustrating. If Microsoft wants to phase out the Control Panel in favor of the Settings app, it should, but there ought to be a single control center on Windows.

Another thing that bothers me is the right-click context menu on Windows 11. If you look at Windows XP, or even the iterations leading up to Windows 10, it was simple. You right-clicked on a file, and everything was right there. On Windows 11, you have a few options, followed by a "Show more options" section that reveals the legacy options. And of late, the primary context menu is so cluttered with AI-centric options that even the context menu from the days of Windows XP feels better in comparison.

And then there are those random OneDrive, Edge, or Copilot prompts. I have disabled all these features, apart from a bunch of others that affect my workflow. And let's not forget bloatware. Windows 11 comes with a lot of apps you don't even need. Windows XP, too, had a few features that I never recall using. There was "Briefcase," the "Tour Windows XP" utility, and Rover and other characters as the search companion. But those weren't as intrusive and were eventually removed from Windows altogether. It was a simple OS that worked naturally without any confusion.

8 essential features Windows 11 is still missing

Microsoft needs to do better

1
By  João Carrasqueira

Windows XP was built around the end-user

Windows 11 revolves around Microsoft's ecosystem

The philosophy behind Windows has changed a lot lately. From being a user-centric OS, Microsoft now treats Windows as part of a larger ecosystem. Again, most of these changes, like Copilot and OneDrive, are things I disable, but the fact that they are so deeply ingrained is what bothers me. Microsoft has also made it more difficult to set up Windows with a local account. It's now forcing users to opt for a Microsoft account. Back in the days of Windows XP, this was never really a concern. The operating system was centered around the PC itself, not Microsoft.

Deals

Save on PCs and peripherals — Deals on Computers & Work Setup

Explore discounts and limited-time offers in Computers & Work Setup deals — save on laptops, desktops, monitors, keyboards, mice, SSDs, RAM, docking stations and other gear to boost speed, reduce bloat, and simplify your workflow. Shop savings now.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The approach makes sense from a business perspective, but for the end user like me, it's a move in the wrong direction. The PC no longer feels like something I control, at least in the way it's set up by default. Given that the vast majority of Windows users are everyday people, you can't really expect them to change all this.

We also can't ignore the toll all this takes on system resources, especially if you are using a low-end PC. I often see complaints about users running low on memory, even with just a handful of active apps. In that sense, too, Windows XP was much simpler, only featuring what you really needed, not any of the additional stuff you often search for ways to disable. Though, to be fair, it was also made for a different era of hardware.

I tried using the software keeping Windows XP alive in 2026

When everything resides on the web, nothing is more important than internet connectivity

20
By  Kazim Alvi

Windows 11 isn't bad, but it's no match for XP's simplicity

I am not saying Windows 11 is bad. It has improved a lot of things. Gaming is one, search is another, and it's also more secure. But Windows XP represented a simpler era, back when users were truly in control. And that's what I liked about it. Because in a bid to make Windows more modern and connect it to other Microsoft services, we have truly lost the simple, direct, and uncomplicated operating system we grew up with.
