Windows 11 comes with a lot of apps you don't even need. Windows XP, too, had a few features that I never recall using. There was "Briefcase," the "Tour Windows XP" utility, and Rover and other characters as the search companion. But those weren't as intrusive and were eventually removed from Windows altogether. It was a simple OS that worked naturally without any confusion.

8 essential features Windows 11 is still missing

Microsoft needs to do better

1
By  João Carrasqueira

Windows XP was built around the end-user

Windows 11 revolves around Microsoft's ecosystem

The philosophy behind Windows has changed a lot lately. From being a user-centric OS, Microsoft now treats Windows as part of a larger ecosystem. Again, most of these changes, like Copilot and OneDrive, are things I disable, but the fact that they are so deeply ingrained is what bothers me. Microsoft has also made it more difficult to set up Windows with a local account. It's now forcing users to opt for a Microsoft account. Back in the days of Windows XP, this was never really a concern. The operating system was centered around the PC itself, not Microsoft.

Deals

Save on PCs and peripherals — Deals on Computers & Work Setup

Explore discounts and limited-time offers in Computers & Work Setup deals — save on laptops, desktops, monitors, keyboards, mice, SSDs, RAM, docking stations and other gear to boost speed, reduce bloat, and simplify your workflow. Shop savings now.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The approach makes sense from a business perspective, but for the end user like me, it's a move in the wrong direction. The PC no longer feels like something I control, at least in the way it's set up by default. Given that the vast majority of Windows users are everyday people, you can't really expect them to change all this.

We also can't ignore the toll all this takes on system resources, especially if you are using a low-end PC. I often see complaints about users running low on memory, even with just a handful of active apps. In that sense, too, Windows XP was much simpler, only featuring what you really needed, not any of the additional stuff you often search for ways to disable. Though, to be fair, it was also made for a different era of hardware.

I tried using the software keeping Windows XP alive in 2026

When everything resides on the web, nothing is more important than internet connectivity

20
By  Kazim Alvi

Windows 11 isn't bad, but it's no match for XP's simplicity

I am not saying Windows 11 is bad. It has improved a lot of things. Gaming is one, search is another, and it's also more secure. But Windows XP represented a simpler era, back when users were truly in control. And that's what I liked about it. Because in a bid to make Windows more modern and connect it to other Microsoft services, we have truly lost the simple, direct, and uncomplicated operating system we grew up with.
item.content:encoded:
Windows XP has been out of support for over 12 years now. Despite that, people still run Windows XP, and many fondly remember it as one of the best Windows iterations to date. It wasn't the most secure or even the most powerful, if we look at it in present terms, but there was something unique about Windows XP that every discussion about Windows somehow circles back to. If you ask me, it was simplicity that made Windows XP a hit.

I agree, there were limitations, far too many to be honest, but the OS was built around the end user. It was designed to cater to us, not push services and apps Microsoft deemed appropriate. It was a much cleaner OS where everything felt practical. The operating system actually listened to you instead of constantly recommending things. I have been on Windows 11 since the early days, and I have never felt the same way about it. It's undoubtedly more modern-looking, more secure, and can do a lot more, but it's no match for Windows XP when I objectively evaluate it on simplicity.

Windows XP was easier to work with

From launching utilities to changing settings

One of the biggest drawbacks with Windows 11, and what Windows XP excelled at, is making everything easier to access. The Start Menu was way more straightforward, and everything you needed was right there. You opened the Start menu, and within a few clicks, you could access almost anything. On Windows 11, the Start menu is somewhat of a mess, though for frequently used built-in utilities, we do have the Quick Links menu, accessible via the Windows + X keys. That's a slight relief.

Moving on to configuring settings, Windows XP was again more straightforward. Every entry within the Control Panel was pretty much self-explanatory, eliminating the need for guesswork. On Windows 11, the Settings app looks more modern, but many important options require more clicks to access than they ideally should on a user-centric operating system. Let's take changing default apps as an example. On Windows XP, you open the Start menu, go to "Set Program Access and Defaults," and then select default programs for entire tasks. In Windows 11, you have to set defaults by file type, which is a major inconvenience for the average user.

As surprising as it may sound, Windows XP was even better at customizing the OS, at least the Start menu. On Windows 11, you have far fewer options. If you ever checked the "Taskbar and Start Menu Properties" on Windows XP, you could choose the size of icons, set the number of frequently accessed programs to be listed, and configure the style in which items appeared. This level of customization is not natively available in Windows 11, and you are forced to use third-party apps to customize the Start menu.

5 ways Windows has gotten worse in recent years

It's not all good news.

13
By  Simon Batt

Everything worked naturally on Windows XP

No constant distractions and less confusion

Even someone who has never used a Windows PC would have no trouble figuring out Windows XP. On Windows 11, it's a different story altogether. My biggest complaint with Windows 11 is the co-existence of the Settings app and Control Panel. I, for one, favor the Control Panel, but the messy integration is what bothers me. You click on something in the Settings, and you are automatically redirected to the Control Panel. Similarly, click an option in Control Panel, and Windows instantly opens Settings. This has reached a point where it starts to feel frustrating. If Microsoft wants to phase out the Control Panel in favor of the Settings app, it should, but there ought to be a single control center on Windows.

Another thing that bothers me is the right-click context menu on Windows 11. If you look at Windows XP, or even the iterations leading up to Windows 10, it was simple. You right-clicked on a file, and everything was right there. On Windows 11, you have a few options, followed by a "Show more options" section that reveals the legacy options. And of late, the primary context menu is so cluttered with AI-centric options that even the context menu from the days of Windows XP feels better in comparison.

And then there are those random OneDrive, Edge, or Copilot prompts. I have disabled all these features, apart from a bunch of others that affect my workflow. And let's not forget bloatware. Windows 11 comes with a lot of apps you don't even need. Windows XP, too, had a few features that I never recall using. There was "Briefcase," the "Tour Windows XP" utility, and Rover and other characters as the search companion. But those weren't as intrusive and were eventually removed from Windows altogether. It was a simple OS that worked naturally without any confusion.

8 essential features Windows 11 is still missing

Microsoft needs to do better

1
By  João Carrasqueira

Windows XP was built around the end-user

Windows 11 revolves around Microsoft's ecosystem

The philosophy behind Windows has changed a lot lately. From being a user-centric OS, Microsoft now treats Windows as part of a larger ecosystem. Again, most of these changes, like Copilot and OneDrive, are things I disable, but the fact that they are so deeply ingrained is what bothers me. Microsoft has also made it more difficult to set up Windows with a local account. It's now forcing users to opt for a Microsoft account. Back in the days of Windows XP, this was never really a concern. The operating system was centered around the PC itself, not Microsoft.

Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The approach makes sense from a business perspective, but for the end user like me, it's a move in the wrong direction. The PC no longer feels like something I control, at least in the way it's set up by default. Given that the vast majority of Windows users are everyday people, you can't really expect them to change all this.

We also can't ignore the toll all this takes on system resources, especially if you are using a low-end PC. I often see complaints about users running low on memory, even with just a handful of active apps. In that sense, too, Windows XP was much simpler, only featuring what you really needed, not any of the additional stuff you often search for ways to disable. Though, to be fair, it was also made for a different era of hardware.

I tried using the software keeping Windows XP alive in 2026

When everything resides on the web, nothing is more important than internet connectivity

20
By  Kazim Alvi

Because in a bid to make Windows more modern and connect it to other Microsoft services, we have truly lost the simple, direct, and uncomplicated operating system we grew up with.

item.title: Your motherboard's fancy specs don't matter nearly as much as you think
item.itunes:title: Your motherboard's fancy specs don't matter nearly as much as you think
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Building and upgrading PCs over the years has taught me one important lesson: motherboard marketing is designed to make almost every feature sound essential. At one point, I convinced myself that paying extra for a premium motherboard would dramatically improve my experience, even when my actual usage barely benefited from those additions. After going through several PC builds, troubleshooting hardware, and realizing which features I genuinely use every day, I’ve become far more selective about what deserves my money.

Modern motherboards are packed with premium extras, and while some of them absolutely matter, others mostly exist to justify higher price tags. I used to chase flagship boards because I thought more features automatically meant a better PC, but eventually I realized that many of those extras had little impact on gaming, productivity, or long-term reliability.

3 reasons I won’t spend more than $300 on a motherboard ever again

Don't blow your PC budget on your motherboard. It's almost never worth it and here's why.

16
By  Andre Revilla

PCIe Gen 5 support everywhere

Most of my hardware still cannot fully use it

One of the biggest motherboard trends over the last few years has been PCIe Gen 5 support. Many premium motherboards advertise multiple Gen 5 M.2 slots and extra Gen 5 expansion lanes as if they are essential for every modern PC build. At first, I used to think skipping those boards meant my system would become outdated quickly, but my experience has been very different.

The reality is that most of my current hardware doesn’t fully take advantage of PCIe Gen 5 speeds. Even many modern graphics cards barely saturate PCIe Gen 4 bandwidth during gaming, and most SSDs already feel extremely fast on Gen 4. Loading games, transferring files, and launching applications do not suddenly become revolutionary just because benchmark numbers are higher. In normal everyday use, I honestly struggle to notice meaningful differences between a Gen 4 SSD and a more expensive Gen 5 one.

What changed my perspective was realizing how much extra money motherboard brands charge for features. Many people rarely use them fully. Boards with extensive Gen 5 support often cost significantly more, and Gen 5 SSDs themselves usually run hotter while also requiring better cooling solutions. Instead of paying extra for bandwidth I barely use, I would rather invest that budget into a better GPU, more storage, or even a quieter cooling setup that improves my overall experience far more noticeably.

Built-in Wi-Fi on desktop motherboards

Ethernet is still my preferred connection

I used to automatically assume onboard Wi-Fi was a must-have feature for any modern motherboard. Manufacturers heavily promote Wi-Fi 6, Wi-Fi 6E, and now Wi-Fi 7 support as premium additions, and for a while, I kept paying extra for boards that included them. Eventually, I realized I almost never used those wireless features on my desktop PC.

My desktop setup stays in one place, which means I still prefer a wired Ethernet connection whenever possible. Ethernet continues to provide the stability and consistency I want, especially when downloading large files, gaming online, or transferring data across my local network. Wi-Fi has improved tremendously over the years, but I still notice more unpredictable latency spikes and occasional connection inconsistencies compared to a direct cable connection.

Another reason I stopped prioritizing built-in Wi-Fi is flexibility. If I genuinely need wireless support later, adding a PCIe Wi-Fi card or USB adapter is usually simple and relatively affordable. That approach often costs less overall than paying motherboard premiums for wireless features I might never use. For people building compact systems or placing their PCs far from routers, onboard Wi-Fi absolutely makes sense, but for my own desktop habits, Ethernet remains the connection I trust most.

Too many USB ports

I only use the same few ports every day

Motherboard manufacturers love advertising huge numbers of USB ports. Some high-end boards look almost absurd with the amount of connectivity packed onto the rear I/O panel. Earlier, I convinced myself that more ports automatically made a motherboard a better value, but after living with several systems, I noticed something funny. I repeatedly used the exact same handful of ports every single day.

My keyboard, mouse, headset, and occasionally an external drive already cover most of my regular USB needs. Even when I connect extra accessories, I rarely come close to filling every available port. In practice, I discovered that having six or more rear USB connections usually provided no real advantage for the way I personally use my PC.

I also realized that convenience matters more than raw quantity. I care far more about having a few well-placed high-speed ports than an overwhelming number I never touch. Front-panel USB ports on my case handle temporary devices perfectly fine, and a powered USB hub can easily expand connectivity if I ever need more options later. Because of that, paying extra for motherboards loaded with excessive USB configurations simply stopped making sense for my setup.

Premium onboard audio

Dedicated gear makes a bigger difference

Premium onboard audio used to be one of those motherboard features that sounded incredibly important to me. Brands would advertise isolated audio circuits, fancy capacitors, upgraded DACs, and gaming-focused sound enhancements, making it seem like expensive motherboards would completely transform audio quality. After trying several of them over the years, I eventually realized the improvements were much smaller than I had expected.

Deals

Save on PC parts with deals for smarter motherboard buys

Skip overpriced flagship boards and find deals that stretch your build budget in Computers & Work Setup. Shop discounts on motherboards, GPUs, SSDs, cooling, networking adapters, and audio gear — practical savings that improve real-world performance.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The biggest change to my listening experience actually came from dedicated external gear rather than motherboard audio upgrades. Once I started using better headphones alongside a proper DAC, the difference became much more noticeable than switching between different premium motherboard audio solutions. Dedicated hardware simply offered cleaner sound, better control, and more flexibility than relying entirely on integrated motherboard components.

That realization changed how I prioritize motherboard budgets. Instead of spending extra money chasing slightly better onboard audio implementations, I would rather buy a solid mid-range motherboard and use the savings toward proper external audio equipment later. For casual users, modern onboard audio is often good enough, and for anyone truly serious about sound quality, dedicated gear usually offers a far more meaningful upgrade path.

I now prioritize practical features over premium marketing

After building and upgrading PCs for years, I have become much more selective about which motherboard features actually deserve extra money. High-end boards still have their place, especially for enthusiasts with very specific needs, but I no longer assume that more premium features automatically create a better everyday experience. In many cases, I found myself paying for capabilities I barely used while overlooking upgrades that would have improved my setup far more noticeably. These days, I focus less on impressive spec sheets and more on the features that genuinely match how I use my computer every day.

The only 8 features to care about on a new motherboard

Everything else will probably be there on a new motherboard

9
By  Tanveer Singh

Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The biggest change to my listening experience actually came from dedicated external gear rather than motherboard audio upgrades. Once I started using better headphones alongside a proper DAC, the difference became much more noticeable than switching between different premium motherboard audio solutions. Dedicated hardware simply offered cleaner sound, better control, and more flexibility than relying entirely on integrated motherboard components.

That realization changed how I prioritize motherboard budgets. Instead of spending extra money chasing slightly better onboard audio implementations, I would rather buy a solid mid-range motherboard and use the savings toward proper external audio equipment later. For casual users, modern onboard audio is often good enough, and for anyone truly serious about sound quality, dedicated gear usually offers a far more meaningful upgrade path.

I now prioritize practical features over premium marketing

After building and upgrading PCs for years, I have become much more selective about which motherboard features actually deserve extra money. These days, I focus less on impressive spec sheets and more on the features that genuinely match how I use my computer every day.
